coredata.dc:title: The fluorescence quenching of rhodamine 6G as an alternative sensing strategy for the quantification of silver and gold nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Torres, María Andrea Molina
Veglia, Alicia Viviana
Pacioni, Natalia Lorena
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanoparticles
Silver nanoparticles
Rhodamine 6G
Fluorescence quenching
Surface river water
Tap water

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Recovery in drinking and surface river water
Rh6G versus RhB as molecular sensors for MNP

a Analytical parameters determined for AuNP@citrate, AgNP@gallic and AgNP@SDDC using Rh6G as the chemical sensor.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Corresponds to the slope of Stern-Volmer plots (SEN = KSV).

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The superscript indicates the average size.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average recovery and relative standard deviation (RSD) calculated from triplicate samples.

head.ce:title: The fluorescence quenching of rhodamine 6G as an alternative sensing strategy for the quantification of silver and gold nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Universidad Nacional de Córdoba, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Departamento de Química Orgánica, Haya de la Torre y Medina Allende s/n, X5000HUA, Ciudad Universitaria, Córdoba, Argentina

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Universidad Nacional de Córdoba, Facultad de Ciencias Químicas, Departamento de Química Orgánica, Haya de la Torre y Medina Allende s/n, X5000HUA, Ciudad Universitaria, Córdoba, Argentina.

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: Haya de la Torre y Medina Allende s/n

ce:section.ce:para: As a consequence of the high applicability of nanomaterials (NM), commercial products that contain NM increased in the last decade. By 2014–2015, around two thousand products in health and personal care, the automotive industry, electronics, toys, accessories, and food-related goods declared NM as constituents, being a high proportion of metal nanoparticles (MNP) [1,2]. These MNP are likely to enter into environmental compartments, including surface water, river, and lakes from different sources like the industry, household, accidental spills, and wastewater treatment plants [3,4]. Due in part to little or no standard analytical procedures for their quantification, the absence of NM monitoring data impacts as a lack of reliable data regarding the environmental and health implications of NM uses, including MNP [5–7]. In this frame, the ‘analytical metrology for nanomaterials’ has become a priority goal as NM are considered ‘special analytes’ because of their unique physical and chemical properties [8–10].

Analytical methods for the quantification of MNP are still scarce, mostly limited to the use of expensive instruments, requiring highly skilled personnel, and time-consuming protocols of separation and pre-concentration steps [7,9–14]. Thus, the development of other analytical strategies suitable for detecting and quantifying MNP, mainly in aqueous systems using simple methods and reducing the costs, is still a challenge [8,10].

Chemical sensing comprises an interesting analytical strategy, implying molecular recognition between the MNP and a sensing probe that provokes changes in analytical signals sensitive to the MNP concentration [10]. For example, Cayuela et al. proposed the quenching of functionalized carbon dots to reach a detection limit of 0.20 nmol L-1 for 13 and 35 nm citrate-stabilized gold nanoparticles (AuNP@citrate) [15], and a methodology based on the inner filter effect of similar fluorescent sensors for detecting 10 nm silver nanoparticles (AgNP@citrate) with a detection limit of 5.17 μmol L-1 [16]. Zuber et al. used the fluorescence enhancement of a synthesized BODIPY for the detection in the 2–100 pmol L-1 concentration range of 5, 20, and 50 nm AuNP@citrate [17]. Our lab reported the use of basic fuchsin as a spectrophotometric molecular sensor for mixtures of 13 nm AuNP@citrate and 52 nm AgNP@citrate at the pmol L-1 level [18].

Rhodamines (Rh) present the following characteristics: commercial availability, absorption and fluorescence emission in the 450–700 nm range, high molar absorption coefficients, and high fluorescence quantum yields [19]. The presence of heteroatoms in their chemical structures facilitates their interaction with metal surfaces [20]. For instance, RhB and Rh6G (Scheme 1 ) are known to interact with different MNP showing remarkable changes in the Rh spectroscopic properties [21–24].

The non-covalent interactions of Rh dyes with the surfaces of MNP produced a marked quenching of the chromophore’s fluorescence due to collisions and photophysical processes like resonance energy transfer (FRET) [25]. Huang et al. employed the fluorescence quenching of RhB by gold nanoparticles for detecting Hg2+ [24], and recently, Su et al. proposed a similar methodology for the quantification of the fungicide carbendazim [26]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is not any report showing the suitability of Rh quenching for sensing different MNP.

In this work, the focus was on three different types of MNP as analytes: AgNP with sizes in the range 10–20 nm, but different ligands like gallic acid (AgNP@gallic) [27] and a Gemini surfactant (AgNP@SDDC) [28], and 22–26 nm AuNP@citrate [29].We developed and validated an analytical method based on the fluorescence quenching of Rh6G as the molecular sensor to quantify the selected MNP.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, Tetrahedron), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 95%, Cicarelli), trisodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7, Anedra), potassium monobasic phosphate (KH2PO4, Merck), tetrachloroauric acid trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O, Aldrich), silver nitrate (AgNO3, BioPack), rhodamine 6G (Rh6G, Aldrich), rhodamine B (RhB, Aldrich), monohydrate gallic acid (C7H6O5·H2O, Merck), sodium chloride (NaCl, Taurus), potassium chloride (KCl, Merck) and potassium bromide (KBr, Merck) were analytical grade and were used as received. Sodium didecamido cystine (SDDC) was a generous gift from Dr. E. Pachón Gómez and Dr. M. Fernández (INFIQC-CONICET). MilliQ water was obtained from a Millipore instrument (resistivity, 25 °C: 18.2 MΩ cm).

All absorption spectra were measured in a UV–vis Shimadzu 1800 spectrophotometer in the 200–800 nm wavelength range using quartz cells (1 cm path-length). The pH was measured in an Orion (Boston, MA, USA) model 720 A pH-meter using a Ross combination pH electrode. The pH-meter was calibrated using standard buffers at pH = 4.008 and 6.994. Transmission Electron Microscopy images were obtained using a TEM-Jeol 1120 electron microscope, 80 kV accelerating voltage on Carbon-coated copper grids (300 mesh, Electron Microscopy). Centrifugation was performed in an Eppendorf Centrifuge 5804. Fluorescence measurements were done using a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent) with a Peltier temperature controller set at 25.0 °C. Data analysis was performed using Origin®, Version 9.0 OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA, or PAST 4.02 [30].

All the syntheses were performed in deionized water. AgNP@SDDC were synthesized as previously reported [28]. Briefly, in an Erlenmeyer flask containing 20 mL of AgNO3 (0.25 mmol L-1) and SDDC (2.5 mmol L-1), six aliquots of 0.1 mL NaBH4 (10.00 mmol L-1) were added every 30 s, under vigorous magnetic stirring and at room temperature. The stirring was stopped 30 s after the last addition of NaBH4. The MNP were then centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force (rcf) of 2057 × g for 30 min. The pellet was resuspended in 4.0 mL MilliQ water.

AgNP@gallic were synthesized as previously described [27]. Briefly, gallic acid (27.3 µmol L-1) was added to an Erlenmeyer flask already containing 0.2 mmol L-1 AgNO3 and 0.74 mmol L-1 NaOH under vigorous magnetic stirring at room temperature. The mixture reacted for 5 min in the dark. Immediately, the solution color became bright yellow. These MNP were centrifuged at a rcf of 8228 × g for 30 min. The pellet was resuspended in 6.0 mL MilliQ water.

For the synthesis of AuNP@citrate, a HAuCl4 aqueous solution (1.32 mmol L-1) was heated at reflux under vigorous stirring. Then, Na3C6H5O7 (3.2 mmol L-1) was added, and the reaction was maintained for 30 min. The solution color turns from yellow to red. Once the mixture was cooled down to room temperature, the MNP were centrifuged at a rcf of 2604 × g for 30 min, and the pellet was resuspended in 6.0 mL MilliQ water.

Nanoparticle size distributions were determined using TEM images of 20 µL colloidal solutions delivered onto 300 mesh carbon-coated copper grids and dried under air. All the images were analyzed using ImageJ® software (W. S. Rasband, ImageJ, U. S. National Institutes of Health, Bethesda, Maryland, USA, http://rsb.info.nih.gov/ij/, 1997–2012) and counting around 200–1000 particles.
The maximum wavelength (λmax) of the surface Plasmon resonance (SPR) band was determined spectrophotometrically.

Stock solutions of both Rh6G and RhB were prepared in water and kept at 4 °C in a fridge until use. These stock solutions were covered with aluminium foil to avoid photodegradation and their concentration checked using UV–vis before running any fluorescence experiment. All the experiments were performed at 25.0 °C in 1.0 cm path length quartz cells.

Solutions of Rh6G (0.40 µmol L-1) and RhB (0.55 µmol L-1) in 0.01 mol L-1 phosphate buffer pH 6.94 (PBS) or in MilliQ water were excited at 526.5 nm (Rh6G) and 554.0 nm (RhB), using 2.5 nm slit widths for the excitation and emission, in the absence and presence of AgNP@SDDC (2.8 – 260 pmol L-1), AgNP@gallic (2.5 – 300 pmol L-1) and AuNP@citrate (2.2 – 95 pmol L-1). The pH of the PBS solution was adjusted to always obtain a 6.94 value. However, it is noticed that for practical purposes the pH can be expressed as 7.0.

Concentrations of MNP were estimated considering polydispersity and size distribution according to the procedure reported in the literature [31]. Emission spectra were recorded in the 560 – 700 nm range for RhB and 530 – 700 nm range for Rh6G. When necessary spectra were corrected for inner filter effects using Eq. (1) [32,33],
(1)
F
corr
=
F
obs
×
2.3
S 2
A
ex
1 -
10
-
S 2
A
ex
×
10
-
S 3
A
em
×
2.3
S 1
A
em
1 -
10
-
S 1
A
em

Where, Fcorr and Fobs are the corrected and observed emission fluorescence intensity, respectively, S1 (0.8 cm), S2 (1.0 cm) and S3 (0.5 cm) are cuvettes dimensions as described in the literature [33,34], Aex and Aem are the absorbances/cm at the excitation and emission wavelengths, correspondingly. For AgNP@gallic, this correction was used when nanoparticle concentrations were higher than 160.0 pmol L-1 and 270.0 pmol L-1, if using Rh6G or RhB, respectively, and for AuNP@citrate, when their concentration was higher than 30.0 pmol L-1. Finally, for AgNP@SDDC no inner filter effect correction was necessary.

The method selectivity for AgNP@SDDC and AuNP@citrate was evaluated using the analysis of interferences. The maximum tolerance is defined as “the interferent-to-analyte concentration ratio giving a result coinciding with the lower or upper limit of the uncertainty interval for a result obtained in the absence of interferences” [35]. For MNP, there is an associated error in the “standards” concentrations estimated to be around 5–10% [31,36,37]. In the absence of appropriate guidelines for metallic nanoparticles analysis in environmental matrices, it would be recommendable to take this fact into account when evaluating selectivity and specificity for MNP. As a consequence, we chose 10% as the limit of tolerance in signal change. Thus, the fluorescence of solutions containing Rh6G (0,4 μmol L-1) and AgNP@SDDC or AuNP@citrate, was measured after de addition of potential interferences such as NaCl, KCl, KBr, HAuCl4, AgNO3, AgNP, and AuNP until the signal showed a variation >10% at least indicated otherwise.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery in drinking and surface river water
ce:section.ce:para: Drinking water was obtained from a tap in the Córdoba city distribution network. Surface river water was collected at two sampling points along the Ctalamochita river, Río Tercero, Argentina (before and after chemical industries indicated as BI-RW and AI-RW, respectively, Fig. S1). All the samples were stored in clean polyethylene bottles and used without any pre-treatment. Blank solutions did not show any signal for AgNP@SDDC, and AuNP@citrate. Then, solutions of AgNP@SDDC, and AuNP@citrate at different levels of concentrations, and containing 10% (v/v) drinking water, BI-RW or AI-RW, and Rh6G (0.40 µmol L-1) were prepared by triplicate and their fluorescence spectra were recorded and analyzed. The concentration levels were chosen to cover the suitable analyte region by testing the method accuracy, mainly at two critical points: near the quantitation limit and near the upper limit of the dynamic range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rh6G versus RhB as molecular sensors for MNP
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned in the Introduction, the MNP selected as analytes were AgNP@gallic, AgNP@SDDC, and two different sizes of AuNP@citrate. They were synthesized and characterized by UV–visible spectroscopy and electronic transmission microscopy (Fig. 1 ). Table S1 shows their main features like average size, SPR wavelength, and full width at half maximum (FWHM).

The Stern-Volmer plots (F0/F vs. [MNP]) followed the expected linear relationship according to eq. (2), where F0 and F are the emission fluorescence in the absence and presence of MNP and, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant which in this case also indicates the calibration sensitivity (SEN, Tables 1 and S2).
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
M N P

According to the current IUPAC recommendations, the limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were determined using Eq. (3) and (4), respectively [44]:
(3)
LOD =
3.3
s x
SEN
1 +
h 0
+
1 I
(4)
LOQ =
10
s x
SEN
1 +
h 0
+
1 I
(5)
h 0
=
MNP
-
cal
2
∑
i = 1
I
M N P
i
-
MNP
-
cal
2

where sx , h0, and I correspond to the blank or residual standard deviation, the leverage for the blank sample (Eq. (5), using
MNP
-
cal
as the mean calibration concentration and
M N P
i
as each nanoparticle concentration used in the curve), and the number of calibration samples, respectively [44].

The determined LODs (Table 1) are in the order [18,45–47] or better [15,16,48]than others reported in the literature for similar MNP (Table 2 ). Although lower LODs have been reported at the femtomolar level, they usually involved methodologies like ICP-MS that require expensive instruments or highly trained personnel compared to our methodology [49–51]. Besides, some of those analytical methods demand several steps, becoming more time-consuming [47,50]

Recoveries at concentration levels in the order of the LOQ and near the upper calibration limit satisfy the quantification of both types of MNP in drinking water and surface river water, without any pre-treatment to the sample. The overall average recovery (
R
¯
) calculated from data in Tables 4 and 5 was used as the accuracy parameter [55], giving
R
¯
values with their relative standard deviations (RSD, in parentheses) in the different matrices. Thus,
R
¯
values of 88% (17%, BI-RW); 90% (11%, Tap water) and 93% (11%, AI-RW) for AuNP26 @citrate and 82% (10%, tap water); 94% (6%, BI-RW) and 83% (12%, AI-RW) for AgNP12 @SDDC were determined. Then, using a two-tail test-t, the null hypothesis (H0: R = 100%) was accepted with 95% confidence indicating the proposed methodology is accurate under these experimental conditions.
These results demonstrated the method’s usefulness for its application in aquatic environmental samples, one of the potential MNP emission into the environment receivers.

María Andrea Molina Torres: Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing - review & editing. Alicia Viviana Veglia: Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition. Natalia Lorena Pacioni: Conceptualization, Supervision, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported in part by grants from the ANPCyT (PICT 2013-1989), the SECYT-UNC (Res. 266/16, 113/17 and 411/18, and the CONICET PIP 2017-2019 11220170100505CO. M.A.M.T is a grateful recipient of graduated fellowship from CONICET. A.V.V. and N.L.P are research members of the CONICET, Argentina.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105645.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
